questions.q: Explain the general characteristics of group 13 elements (Boron group). Discuss the trends in their physical properties, and also give reasons for their chemical reactivity.
questions.answer: The general characteristics of group 13 elements are: (a) Their atomic and ionic radii increase down the group due to the increase in the number of energy levels. (b) The electronegativity of these elements decreases down the group. (c) The metallic character increases down the group. (d) The melting points and boiling points of these elements decrease down the group. (e) The metallic character increases down the group. These elements exhibit +3 oxidation state due to the presence of three unpaired electrons in the s-orbital of the outermost shell. The reactivity of these elements increases down the group. This is due to the decrease in the energy required for the removal of the third electron from the valence shell of the elements. They react with halogens to form trihalides. They also react with water to form oxoacids, such as borax and boric acid. Boron also forms other compounds like boron carbide and boron nitride.
marking_scheme: Explain the general characteristics of group 13 elements (1 mark)
Discuss the trends in their physical properties (1 mark)
Give reasons for their chemical reactivity (1 mark)
Discuss the formation of compounds like borax and boric acid (1 mark)
Mention any two other compounds formed by boron (1 mark)

questions.q: What are the differences between the chemistry of aluminium and boron? Provide examples to support your answer.
questions.answer: The chemistry of aluminium and boron differs in many ways. Aluminium is a highly reactive metal, whereas boron is a metalloid. Aluminium readily loses 3 electrons to form a stable +3 ion, whereas boron does not exhibit a stable +3 ion due to the presence of a half-filled p-orbital. Aluminium reacts with acids to produce hydrogen gas, whereas boron does not react with acids. Aluminium reacts with oxygen to form a thin layer of aluminium oxide, whereas boron reacts with oxygen to form boron trioxide. Aluminium is used in the manufacture of aircraft and other high-strength alloys, whereas boron is used in the manufacture of glass and ceramics.
marking_scheme: Explain the reactivity of aluminium and boron (1 mark)
Compare the reactions of aluminium and boron with acids (1 mark)
Discuss the formation of aluminium and boron oxides (1 mark)
Mention the uses of aluminium and boron (1 mark)
Provide any two other differences between the chemistry of aluminium and boron (1 mark)
questions.topic: Differences Between the Chemistry of Aluminium and Boron

questions.q: Explain the structure and properties of white phosphorus. How does it react with air and water?
questions.answer: White phosphorus is a waxy, white, highly reactive solid. It is made up of four phosphorus atoms that are linked together in a tetrahedral structure. The structure of white phosphorus is P4. It has a melting point of 44°C and a boiling point of 280°C. White phosphorus is highly reactive due to the presence of free electrons in the molecule. It reacts with air to form phosphorus pentoxide, a highly oxidizing agent. It also reacts with water to form phosphorus acid, a weak acid. White phosphorus is highly toxic and can cause irritation to the skin and eyes.
marking_scheme: Explain the structure of white phosphorus (1 mark)
Discuss the properties of white phosphorus (1 mark)
Describe the reaction of white phosphorus with air (1 mark)
Describe the reaction of white phosphorus with water (1 mark)
Mention the toxicity of white phosphorus (1 mark)
questions.topic: Structure and Properties of White Phosphorus

questions.q: What are the important uses of tin and lead? Discuss their extraction, properties, and applications.
questions.answer: Tin and lead are two important metals used in various industries. Tin is extracted from cassiterite, a tin ore, through the process of reduction. It has a low melting point of 231.9°C and a high boiling point of 2602°C. Tin is used in the manufacture of soldering materials, tin cans, and bearings. Lead is extracted from galena, a lead ore, through the process of reduction. It has a low melting point of 327.5°C and a high boiling point of 1750°C. Lead is used in the manufacture of batteries, pipes, and radiation shielding. Both tin and lead are highly malleable and ductile, making them useful in various applications.
marking_scheme: Discuss the extraction of tin and lead (1 mark)
Describe the properties of tin and lead (1 mark)
Mention the uses of tin (1 mark)
Mention the uses of lead (1 mark)
Compare the properties and uses of tin and lead (1 mark)

questions.q: Compare the chemistry of carbon and silicon. Discuss the similarities and differences in their physical properties, chemical reactivity, and applications.
questions.answer: Carbon and silicon are two elements that belong to the same group in the periodic table. However, their chemistry differs in many ways. Carbon has a high electronegativity and a small atomic size, which makes it highly reactive. Silicon has a lower electronegativity and a larger atomic size, which makes it less reactive. Carbon forms a wide variety of compounds, including alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes. Silicon also forms a variety of compounds, including silicates and silanes. Both carbon and silicon are able to form long chains and rings, but silicon is more able to form three-dimensional networks. Carbon is used in the manufacture of plastics, fuels, and fibers, whereas silicon is used in the manufacture of semiconductors, glass, and ceramics.
marking_scheme: Discuss the similarities in the chemistry of carbon and silicon (1 mark)
Discuss the differences in the chemistry of carbon and silicon (1 mark)
Compare the physical properties of carbon and silicon (1 mark)
Compare the chemical reactivity of carbon and silicon (1 mark)
Mention the applications of carbon and silicon (1 mark)
questions.topic: Comparison of the Chemistry of Carbon and Silicon
